Africa

Cheetah

Acinonyx jubatus
Status Vulnerable
Habitat Savannahs, Grasslands, Open Plains
Diet Carnivore
Lifespan 10-12 years (wild)
Weight 21-72 kg
×

The cheetah (Acinonyx jubatus) stands as the world's fastest land animal, a specialized speed machine built for explosive acceleration and high-velocity pursuit. Capable of reaching speeds up to 112 km/h (70 mph) in mere seconds, these elegant predators represent the pinnacle of evolutionary adaptation for cursorial hunting. Unlike other big cats, cheetahs hunt primarily during daylight hours, relying on exceptional eyesight to spot prey from elevated vantage points. Their distinctive tear marks running from eyes to mouth reduce glare and enhance focus during high-speed chases. With fewer than 7,100 individuals remaining in the wild, the cheetah faces an uncertain future despite its remarkable adaptations.

Habitat & Range

Cheetahs inhabit the open savannas, grasslands, and semi-arid plains of sub-Saharan Africa, with a small remnant population in Iran. They require large home ranges with abundant prey and minimal tree cover that would obstruct their high-speed pursuits. East African strongholds include the Serengeti-Mara ecosystem, Ruaha National Park, and Tsavo Conservation Area. Unlike other big cats, cheetahs avoid dense woodland and forest, preferring areas where they can use elevated termite mounds or rocks as lookout points to spot prey across open terrain.

Behavior & Social Structure

Cheetahs are primarily solitary animals, with females raising cubs alone and males forming small coalitions, usually of brothers. They are unique among big cats for hunting during daylight hours, typically early morning and late afternoon. Their hunting technique involves stalking to within 100 meters of prey, then exploding into a high-speed pursuit lasting 20-30 seconds. After a successful kill, they must eat quickly before larger predators like lions and hyenas steal their catch. Cheetahs communicate through various vocalizations including chirps, purrs, and hisses, but cannot roar like other big cats.

Diet & Nutrition

As specialized carnivores, cheetahs primarily hunt medium-sized ungulates weighing 20-60 kg, with Thomson's gazelle being their preferred prey in East Africa. They also take impala, springbok, and young wildebeest. Unlike other predators, cheetahs rarely scavenge and depend entirely on fresh kills. A single adult requires approximately 3-4 kg of meat daily. Their hunting strategy relies on sight rather than smell, using elevated positions to scan for prey before stalking and sprinting. The high energy cost of their hunting style means they need to rest frequently between attempts.

Key Facts

  • Fastest land animal, reaching speeds up to 112 km/h (70 mph)
  • Can accelerate from 0-100 km/h in under 3 seconds
  • Non-retractable claws act like cleats for traction during pursuit
  • Achieves hunting success rate of 40-50%, highest among big cats
  • Distinctive 'tear marks' reduce sun glare during daytime hunting
  • Flexible spine and long tail provide stability during high-speed turns

Conservation Status

Classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN Red List, though some scientists argue for Endangered status. With fewer than 7,100 individuals remaining across Africa and a small Iranian population, cheetahs have disappeared from over 90% of their historic range. East Africa hosts significant populations, particularly in the Serengeti-Mara ecosystem. Primary threats include habitat loss, human-wildlife conflict, prey depletion, and genetic bottleneck reducing population diversity. Conservation efforts focus on protecting remaining habitat, mitigating conflict with livestock farmers, and maintaining prey populations in key strongholds.

Primary Threats

  • Habitat loss and fragmentation reducing hunting grounds
  • Human-wildlife conflict with livestock farmers
  • Prey depletion from bushmeat poaching
  • Competition with larger predators (lions, hyenas) for kills
  • Genetic bottleneck reducing disease resistance
  • Illegal wildlife trade for exotic pets

Related Species

🦁 Lion

Frequently Asked Questions

How big is an Cheetah?

Cheetahs typically weigh 21-72 kg and can be identified by their distinctive features. They are native to Savannahs, Grasslands, Open Plains.

Are Cheetahs endangered?

The Cheetah is currently classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN Red List. Classified as Vulnerable by the IUCN Red List, though some scientists argue for Endangered status. With fewer than 7,100 individuals remaining across ...

What do Cheetahs eat?

Cheetahs are carnivore. As specialized carnivores, cheetahs primarily hunt medium-sized ungulates weighing 20-60 kg, with Thomson's gazelle bein...

How long do Cheetahs live?

In the wild, Cheetahs typically live for 10-12 years (wild). Their lifespan can vary based on environmental conditions and conservation efforts.
